petitioner_background: The petitioner is a ship modeler seeking classification as an employment-based immigrant under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act as an alien of extraordinary ability. The approval of the employment-based immigrant visa petition was previously revoked by the Director of the Texas Service Center on March 10, 2011, and the director dismissed the petitioner's motion to reopen on May 13, 2011. No substantive details about the petitioner's career or qualifications are discussed in this decision, as it addresses only the procedural dismissal of the appeal.
outcome_reasoning: The AAO summarily dismissed the appeal because counsel failed to specifically identify any erroneous conclusion of law or statement of fact as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.3(a)(1)(v). Counsel indicated on Form I-290B and in an accompanying cover letter that a brief and supporting documents would be submitted within 30 days of the June 13, 2011 appeal filing, but approximately nine months later the AAO had received nothing further. Because counsel did not specifically address the reasons stated for revocation, did not provide any additional evidence, and failed to provide any specific statement or argument regarding the basis of the appeal, summary dismissal was required.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Procedural dismissal: Counsel filed a Form I-290B appeal on June 13, 2011, stating that a legal brief and supporting documents would be submitted within 30 days, but nine months later no brief or additional evidence had been received. Under 8 C.F.R. § 103.3(a)(1)(v), an appeal must be summarily dismissed if the party fails to specifically identify any erroneous conclusion of law or statement of fact. Counsel did not specifically address the reasons for revocation, provided no additional evidence, and offered no specific statement or argument regarding the basis of the appeal, resulting in summary dismissal.
